segments.text: The E-Day Plus 350 The National Broadcasting Company makes its facilities
available for a special program, the E-Day Plus 350 and the behalf of the United Jewish
appeal for refugees, overseas needs, and Palestine, featuring Quentin Reynolds, noted bar and
correspondent, and Lady Christians, is the English star of stage and screened in an original
radio play. The honorable Henry Morgan Ford Jr. will be heard in a brief address. The United
Jewish Appeal provides the funds for a worldwide program of relief, rehabilitation, and resettlement
of the 1,400,000 Jewish survivors of Hitlerism.
The constituent agencies of the United Jewish Appeal,
which carry on this humanitarian effort,
are the Joint Distribution Committee,
the United Palestine Appeal,
and the National Refugee Service.
Good evening. This is Quentin Reynolds reporting.
Today is V.E. Day plus 350.
350 days since we smashed the Nazis into submission.
In those 350 days since the E-Day, peace has come to America.
And with it the opportunity for a lasting peace this time.
But as we look around the world, what do we find?
The gaping wounds of war are still bleeding.
Yes, and the casualties are still mounting.
Europe has been ripped by a giant rake leaving wreckage and ruins everywhere.
In the backwash of war came famine and pestilence, homelessness and new hates that made breathe the next war.
We do not read for the dead, they're beyond help.
We read for the living as the breath goes out of them before health reaches them.
At the rubble in the ruin that is Europe today,
I've seen Hubble together a handful of homeless,
ruthless, despairing Jews.
They were soldiers too, soldiers without weapons.
I walked through the muck in the mire of Hitler's inferno.
I saw the victims, the dead in the living dead.
And I smelled a foul and a scrapable stench of the death factories.
Here is the roll call.
My Daneck, Auschwitz, Dachau, Buchenwald,
Beg in Belsen.
The graveyards of six million Jews, soldiers of democracy.
Six out of seven Jews perished in Europe and more are still dying.
cut down by Hitler's rear guard, hunger, homelessness, despair, and disease.
On Beeday Plus 350, there is still the grim danger that our inaction
may finish Hitler's job of extermination. On Beeday Plus 350,
there are 100,000 Jews in displaced persons camps.
Prisons without bars.
I lived through the battle of the Warsaw ghetto. We fought them off for 53 days.
Finally they smashed us with tanks, with flamethrowers, with bombs.
A few of us escaped out of thousands.
And now I am here, a displaced person.
I tried to go back to my old home. When I got there they drove me out.
So here I am again. Is this camp the only place in the world where I can be safe?
As the world forgotten us, there only a few of us left.
But we too share the fate of our martyred brothers now that peace has come.
That is what I heard in Bergen-Belson, liberated one year ago on the day that Franklin Delano rose what was buried.
Today, V.E. Day plus 350, 10,000 homeless Jews remain in these barracks of grim memories.
My name is Rachel, once there were six of us.
We lived in Vienna.
My father was an engineer.
My mother took care of our home.
My older sister has been lived with us.
I had a young brother.
Have you ever dreamed of the tune of a Viennese voice?
Is it possible that he once did such a thing?
He had music in our concentration camp, too.
The urge of death.
What happened to my family, don't you know?
They're all gone, murdered by the Nazi.
The war is over.
I'm the only one left.
But I'm still in the same camp by the Nazis kept me.
How long must I stay here?
I have no family, no home.
I cannot go back to the country where my people were murdered.
Where should I go?
Don't lose hope, Rachel. There are people who want you.
They will rescue you.
Help is coming from America to keep you and the others alive.
Food, clothing and shelter will reach you.
Let me take you to a JDC farm school in Holland
where thousands of young people like you are preparing for life in a free land.
We dance the horror to this tune.
It's the dance of the Palestine Pioneers.
We dance it after school, after work.
I'm living again.
There's health and there's hope.
I'm learning all about farming, gardens, vineyards, livestock, poultry.
There are many of us here from Austria, Romania, Poland, France, Hungary, Holland, and Belgium.
We are training to become pioneers in Palestine.
That is the land we are dreaming of.
The promised land of our ancestors.
There we'll be able to forget what our eyes have seen, what our bodies have suffered,
But what our souls have sorrowed, but it is too much hope for me.
All of us yearned to go there, but there are many obstacles,
and I'm not sure that I shall be one of the lucky ones.
Curricut, Rachel, your friends in America will not forsake you.
Tens of thousands have already been helped to go to Palestine.
Attention, land off the port bow.
It's really true. We are nearing the port of Haifa. I'm afraid I shall wake up and find myself
back in Bergen-Belden. But no, this is no dream. I can see the faint outlines of the hills
of Lebanon, and in my eyes I can see the crowds at the dock waiting to welcome us. Here
in this world, in this new land, as your work has never been fought,
we will till the soil, we will share the fruits of our labor
and our learning with all the peoples of the world.
But my heart thinks when I think of those who are not as lucky as I am.
It's true that many will be left behind,
would be our job to keep their hope alive and give them a new start in other lands.
For example, there will be a welcome for thousands right here in our own United States.
The first of these displaced persons will arrive next month under the regular immigration quotas.
Preparations have already been made to receive them and give them a new life here.
These are the day-to-day problems of the United Jewish appeal.
to care for those who find a haven in the United States,
to provide for those who go to Palestine,
to sustain the lives and hopes of those who remain in Europe.
Many thousands have been saved,
but one of the hundreds of thousands in Europe
that still have to be helped.
I can't forget the voice of the man in Bergen Belson.
Has the world forgotten us?
They're only a few of us left, but we too share the fate of our martyred brothers.
And now we present the Honorable Henry Morgan Thaw Jr., former Secretary of the Treasury.
In a few days we shall celebrate the first anniversary of the E Day.
To recapture some of the exaltation and joy which greeted the Allied victory over hate,
intolerance, cruelty, and all the other aspects of Nazism, there'll be many ceremonies,
parades, and I fear, bankwits.
We will recall with justifiable pride the decisive role which America played in saving
the world from slavery and destruction.
But before we congratulate ourselves, let us look across the Atlantic and see what victory
has achieved for civilization.
One year after victory, a war of survival is still being fought in Europe.
The armies have left the battlefield, but in their place stand millions of human beings
desperately seeking to defend themselves against the forces of hunger, disease, homelessness
and hate. One year after victory, human beings are still dying of starvation. One year after
victory, human beings are still cringing in fear. One year after victory, human beings
are still struggling to escape the poison of Nazism that rises over Hitler's grave
to pollute the atmosphere and stain the good name of the world.
Millions died in Hitler's death house.
Millions suffered in his torture chambers,
but no people suffered so much as the Jews.
The six million who died in the crematories and gas chambers
were victims of a crime against humanity
too vast for the human mind to comprehend.
The mass graves of the Jewish martyrs are scattered over the face of war-ravaged Europe.
Of the Jews who fell into Hitler's clutches, only 1,400,000 survived.
The children were the first in most ruthlessly hunted victims.
Only one out of ten Jewish youngsters remains alive today.
Has one year of liberation brought these survivors of freedom in a security which they longed and hoped for?
The answer can be found in recent incidents in Austria and other parts of Europe.
It is unbelievable, but it is true that one year after liberation, long after the destruction of the Nazi
death sentences. The horrifying cry of two of the gas chambers can still be heard in public.
Governments have been unable to cope with the anti-Semitism,
planted and cultivated by the Nazis. Men and women seeking to return to their own homes
have been driven away by demonstrations of hatred. The situation of the Jewish survivors in
Europe is tragic beyond description. They are in desperate physical need. Despair is depleting
their spiritual strength. Many thousands of them plead for a place that they can call home.
Large numbers of them turning, longingly, towards Palestine as the one land that can deliver them
And for all time from the fears and anxieties of wandering and oppression.
Others with relatives and friends in the United States want to come here in accordance
with President Truman's directive to facilitate the immigration of displaced persons.
To assist both these groups and all the others, an unparalleled task in human reconstruction
must be carried on.
We seek to do it through the $100 million campaign of the United Jewish Appeal for refugees,
overseas needs, and Palestine.
The problem of saving the remnants of European jury is one of the most urgent questions facing
the architects of the new world.
The foundations of the structure of the world of tomorrow will be as sound as its provisions
for homeless and oppressed minorities.
The cause of its purposes are so closely linked with the attainment of true justice and human
liberty for all mankind.
United Jewish appeal deserves a support of every American.
Thank you, Mr. Morgenthau.
You have heard BE Day Plus 350, a special broadcast featuring Quentin Reynolds and Mady Christians
presented in behalf of the United Jewish Appeal for Refugees, Overseas Needs, and Palestine.
Copies, if they talk by the Honorable Henry Morgan Thor, or the original dramatization heard on this program,
may be obtained by writing to the United Jewish Appeal at 342 Madison Avenue, New York.
The dance of the Palestine Pioneers appears in songs of Zion compiled by Harry Cooperismitt.
This is NBC, the National Broadcasting Company.
